author: @nunyabidniz2868
text: The reason for the higher price on British nationals taking out a patent in the 1836 revamp was that at that time, the UK patent system would invalidate an applicant for a patent in the UK if a patent had previously been granted anywhere else in the world, so you had to take out a British patent _first_ if you *ever* expected to protect your invention w/in the UK (the richest, most industrialized country in the world at the time.) So you might expect to see some pushback, esp. as the U.S. and the UK were still rather hostile to each other given that many people who fought in the War of 1812 were still alive in the 1830s. And of course, the higher price for any international applicants is to be expected since they could always have arranged for a US agent [lawyer] to take one out for them; lawyers have always had a strong union and good lobbyists protecting their income streams...

author: @rblom1632
text:
Hi Robert, I like to know how to make money out of good ideas.. To me it sometimes feels impossible. It seems to me as if you invent a light bulb today, they copy it in China tomorrow.. They just steal it! 

I think 3d printing is a very nice inventors tool. Especially if you can make molds and cast series of 100.. To start a business.. A few molds a website.. And a PATENT.

Otherwise you have to invent stuff for free.. If it does not work it's your wasted time and if it works, big business steals it? To me it also seems like today you need ten lawyers to file a patent.. And another ten if a big business wants to get the benefits of your work without paying for it.. 

How can I make money out of a good idea?

I actually like the idea of very long lasting patents. As solid ground of a family business.. To me it seems fair to pay money to the grandchildren of Edison, for the invention of the light bulb.

Farmers pass their land to their children. Inventors should pass their patents to their children..

To protect family businesses.

Does China pay patent money to produce light bulbs? They did not invent it! Why not? Let em pay..

author: @AnonymousAnarchist2
text:
I agree with the benifits of paitent models. Ernestly I believe there should accessable libraries of models for inventions past present and future for all to browse, and an ability to learn from, improve on and advance using the paitent system rather then any paitent being a roadblock or any paitent law being a stopping point for production.

Yes that means I do not belive in any monoply on production, but that does not mean I think people should not get paid. 

To use a distinctly American example, if the engineers and designers gots paid, do you think anyone would buy a Harley over the Honda copy? I dont. Honda has a great history of reducing manufacturing costs by clever automation. Harley is too mired in tradtion to break free and use many of these new manufacturing methods; and some elements of thier design must remain; but they do have excellent engineers who take those shortcomings and sometimes even make them strengths.
I think most would ride a honda made harley.. and if they had the funds buy a harley made gas tank and fenders because they do look a little nicer.

author: @CCoburn3
text: Here is an interesting thing about patent models. During the War of 1812, the British burned Washington -- except for one building. When all of the rest of the government scampered off to safety, the head of the Patent Office stayed. He took the British commander on a tour of the Patent Office and showed him the models. He convinced the British commander that burning these models would be a detriment to all mankind. So the British guarded the Patent Office, and it was the only government building they didn't burn.

author: @CrudelyMade
text: Economists have estimated that we would be 70 years ahead in our technology if it wasn't for the current patent system.

replies.author: @paul1979uk2000
replies.text:
It wouldn't surprise me, the patent system makes it much harder for smaller individuals or companies with less resources to compete with bigger ones that can throw money at it.

I also feel that bigger companies use the patent system to make it harder for smaller ones to innovate, which reduces competition, bumps up the price across almost all goods and potentially, creates a lot more monopolise around the world.

When it comes to consumers, I feel that the more ground up companies that can innovate and create things, the more competition it will bring, which should over the long run mean more choice and lower prices.

The patent system is being corrupted to such a level that it's becoming more difficult for the small guy to compete, meaning fewer companies and individuals end up running everything, the current system is clearly holding back progress and needs major reforms but I suspect the US would be very hostile to any change because they stand to lose the most from it, especially at a corporate level, but either way, I think change is coming regardless of resistance.

Still, things could be worse, look at what they tried to do with software patents a few years ago lol and with the likes of Apple trying to patent the rectangular phone shape, imagine if they actually won that and what that would mean for rivals in the smartphone industry trying to compete.

So a major overall is needed on the patent system and it's highly unlikely to come from the US because the system is bought and sold a long time ago by corporations that don't care about public interest and the government seems too weak to stand up to them, meaning, it's going to have to be an outsider that changes it, and the only credible ones are the EU and China and China doesn't look too appealing to me for doing that change and the EU has been willing to do more radical changes that the US isn't willing.

replies.author: @CrudelyMade
replies.text:
​@paul1979uk2000 an excellent example of the stagnation caused by patents is the 3d printing world. 
when the 70s and 80s patents expired around 2014 the industry boomed and 10 years later we have $300 printers that do amazing prints right at home. 
compare that to the 3d printing market from 1980 to 2014.  not a peep, not much advancement.
and now kids are learning cad and design, engineers are iterating daily instead of monthly. 
it's really a pity the govt answers to the $ instead of the people.  ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

Not necessarily, Patents stop reproduction of ideas however there more important purpose (in my opinion) which is to allow others to study your invention and perhaps improve on it  and is why patents are freely published. All patents must refer to previous patents. That is to show what you used as inspiration. It also allows the original inventor to get their due.

Its the courts that are the ones holding back progress, particularly one court in Texas.


BTW As a discipline Economists have a woeful record of getting it right.

author: @saiello2061
text: Much as we'd like the idea of protecting one's invention for financial and/or for the kudos, patent systems are fraught with pitfalls and expense, you can never have true protection. There is another patent "model" that at least the UK patent office provides that most are unaware of and could better suit the home inventor. For a few hundred pounds you can submit your idea and as soon as it lands on the desk of the patent office it has full patent status regardless as to whether its a goer or not. The downside is that it only lasts for a year, after which it's up for grabs. A year should be enough time to make good on your invention, either to pursue it yourself or sell on. Failing that, you could always make slight alterations to your design and re-patent it as a new design for another year.

author: @TomFarrell-p9z
text:
Thinking of the two ideas I had that my company applied for patents for:  For one, a mirror system to steer collimated optical beams, submitting a model would have been a fine idea, although heavy to build and ship, while keeping aligned.  (I actually had built a model on a lab bench.  Not for shipping.)  Engineering a model to be shipped would have probably been cost prohibitive, but it was in Los Angeles, and a policy of periodically sending a patent examiner to the hundreds of labs to examine the models may have had merit.

For the other idea, adjusting the data rate of a laser communications system in real time based on the atmospheric turbulence between the transmitter and receiver, a model would have been pointless.  It's clear how the idea works, but making it work in turbulence would have introduced a host of other problems, and modeling the turbulence in the lab would have been an interesting engineering exercise by itself.  If you could build a working model, you would have had a "full scale" system.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: interesting post mate - thanks for sharing

author: @philipvecchio3292
text:
It seems like building the model as part of the patent process would also help tamp down some of the patent trolls. 

There's some interesting libertarian thinkers who disagree with the idea of intellectual property because how could a person own an idea?  Coca-Cola, for example, hasn't patented or published their recipe, they keep it locked up in Atlanta because after 100 years they would have to give their recipe to everyone. The patent protection would actually reveal their process in the long run. 

At the same point, an idea is the most valuable when it's spread. Two people Can know how to make a peanut butter and jelly sandwich without the other one being poorer. The intellectual property process can keep an idea from coming to Market longer than it should. 

It doesn't mean that an inventor shouldn't be rewarded for their idea, but maybe there's a different way that can happen while still allowing the free-flowing of ideas.

replies.text: This is probably the only way out of this mess.

author: @you-know-who.
text: Would you consider covering the dark side of the patent system? The fact that they can dismiss a patent because it would disturb the economy bearing in mind that the economy is based around oil and gas. And the fact that military can confiscate patents at there own discretion.

replies.author: @CCoburn3
replies.text: Nice myth. But completely untrue. The Patent Office doesn't "dismiss" patent applications. They may reject applications when the invention is nonfunctional, drawn to a judicial exception, or if it is taught by the prior art. Most of the things people say are rejected because they might "disturb the economy," violate the laws of physics -- usually the 2nd law of thermodynamics. Or they claim chemical methods of producing nuclear fusion. Nor can the military "confiscate" patents. Though they can classify the subject matter of a patent.

author: @SimonPlatten
text: I took out a patent in 1987, at a cost I could afford, then a year later I was told I would need an international patent which is very much more expensive and more than I could afford.  So I parked my idea, if you look at my designs and the patent you could say it was a iPad back in 1987…. But if you can’t afford it, then it’s all geared up to those that can.
